taskDescription: Drag the correct anatomical terms into the blanks to complete statements about the pectoral region and breast.
textField:
Pectoralis major has three heads of origin: the *clavicular* head, the *sternocostal* head, and the abdominal head. It is a powerful adductor, medial rotator, and flexor of the arm at the shoulder joint.

All three heads of pectoralis major converge to insert on the lateral lip of the *bicipital groove* of the humerus. The tendon has a characteristic *U-shaped* fold where the lower fibres insert higher than the upper fibres.

Pectoralis minor lies deep to pectoralis major and inserts on the *coracoid* process of the scapula. It acts as an accessory muscle of *respiration* by elevating the ribs when the scapula is fixed.

The clavicular head of pectoralis major is supplied by the *lateral pectoral* nerve, while the sternocostal head receives supply from both the lateral and *medial pectoral* nerves. The medial pectoral nerve pierces pectoralis *minor* to reach pectoralis major.

The most common route of lymphatic drainage of the breast is via the *axillary* lymph nodes, which receive about *75* percent of the drainage. The *internal mammary* (parasternal) nodes receive most of the remaining drainage.

overallFeedback.feedback: Review the anatomy of the pectoral region.
Good effort! Review the details you missed.
